product_description_under_30_words: Forty-page picture book posing twenty open questions with no single right answer, illustrated by Christian Robinson.
ember_verdict: Your nearly-three is past naming things — they want to know why and what if. Each page is a proper question with room to explain, disagree and spin a story. Not a sleepy read.
personalization_hint: likely to ask why and what happens next rather than just point and name
why_it_fits: Open-ended questions invite explanation and prediction — the 34–36m shift beyond 31–33m simple story recall.
caveats: BookTrust interest age 4-5 (Walker lists 3-7) — fine for chatty nearly-threes with an adult; may buzz brains at bedtime.
gift_note: Strong gift for a chatty nearly-three who likes having opinions.
ownership_note: Fresh 2024 paperback — unlikely duplicate on most shelves.
rating_source: LoveReading4Kids / editorial aggregate (Amazon live page bot-blocked)
review_quality_note: Retailer/community mix; live page bot-blocked on check date.
preloved_signal_note: Picture books hold up well second-hand.

product_description_under_30_words: Ladybird picture book with sixty-plus story branches — the child picks what happens next on every spread.
ember_verdict: If your child already narrates the shopping trip, this hands them the plot. They choose, explain why, and tell you what happens next — without you turning pages into a quiz.
personalization_hint: likely to invent stories from everyday moments and want to decide what happens
why_it_fits: Choice-led branching plots let the child lead and explain — distinct from 31–33m books where adults still drive the story.
caveats: Newer 2024 title — thinner review base than You Choose.
gift_note: Fresh alternative if the family already owns You Choose.
ownership_note: Low duplication risk — newer than the You Choose shelf staple.
review_quality_note: Emerging review count; strong publisher/editorial support.

product_description_under_30_words: Choice-filled picture book where children pick people, places, outfits and adventures on every spread.
ember_verdict: Still the cleanest fit when your child wants to answer and explain on every page. Borrow first if you already own it — many families do.
personalization_hint: likely to want to pick, justify choices and keep the conversation going across spreads
why_it_fits: Choice-led pages are built for what-next and why talk — the category benchmark at nearly three.
caveats: High ownership risk; not a fresh buy for many UK families.
gift_note: Only gift if you know they do not already have a copy.
ownership_note: Borrow or bring back out — many nurseries and grandparents already own this.
review_quality_note: Retailer/community mix; live page bot-blocked on check date.
preloved_signal_note: Ubiquitous second-hand — check condition of well-thumbed copies.
evidence_notes: Multiple ISBNs in print (incl. 9780241356081 paperback, 9780241764671 board Aug 2025). Amazon ASIN may vary by edition.

product_description_under_30_words: Classic picture book of silly and serious would-you-rather dilemmas with no wrong answers.
ember_verdict: BookTrust pairs this with Twenty Questions for a reason — your child has to pick, explain why, and sometimes change their mind. Brilliant when they are starting proper because-answers.
personalization_hint: likely to argue their case and explain preferences out loud
why_it_fits: Call-and-response choices demand explanation — matches nearly-three talk-back without a passive plot.
caveats: Some spreads are deliberately yucky — lean into giggles, skip pages if squeamish.
gift_note: Good library borrow; buy if they ask for it twice.
ownership_note: Classic but less universal than Gruffalo — still check the shelf first.
review_quality_note: Retailer/community mix; live page bot-blocked on check date.
preloved_signal_note: Picture books hold up well second-hand.

product_name: Don't Let the Pigeon Drive the Bus!
product_description_under_30_words: Comic picture book where a pleading pigeon asks the reader to let him drive — and the child must answer.
ember_verdict: Every spread gives your child a job: say no, explain why, negotiate. Less choice-led than You Choose, but perfect when they want to talk back with confidence.
personalization_hint: likely to enjoy answering back and explaining rules to characters
why_it_fits: Call-and-response dialogue lets the child lead the conversation — active talk-back, not passive listening.
caveats: High-energy — not the calmest bedtime pick.
gift_note: Fun gift for a child who likes being in on the joke.
ownership_note: Popular classic — borrow first if you already have Mo Willems titles.
review_quality_note: Retailer/community mix; live page bot-blocked on check date.
preloved_signal_note: Picture books hold up well second-hand.
evidence_notes: Walker live page verified for board ISBN 9781406386073 at £7.99. Paperback ASIN 1844285136 retained as alternate; confirm preferred format before ingest.

why_it_fits: Gentle guessing-game plot — child can predict Olive's answer from visual clues.
caveats: Nov 2024 title — thin Amazon review base; strong Kirkus/Horn Book support.

why_it_fits: Reissued Just Imagine — imaginative what-if choices when You Choose feels familiar.
caveats: Same format as You Choose — skip if you want variety.

why_it_fits: Repeated is-it-a-shark? prediction loop — child explains what they see through the telescope.

why_it_fits: Suspenseful repeat structure — child predicts whether the plan will work.
caveats: Less explicitly question-led than Top 5 — strong prediction backup.

why_it_fits: Visual guessing — child explains who each animal might be and what happens next.
caveats: Gentler pace — good for quieter explainers.

why_it_fits: Deadpan dialogue — child can explain who took the hat and what the bear should do.

why_it_fits: Rhyme prediction — what sits on what, and why Frog is cross.

why_it_fits: Sequel dilemmas when the original Would You Rather is worn thin.

why_it_fits: Forces the reader to say silly words — child explains why it is funny.
